summarization.a: shows three DFT-optimized adsorption geometries of aniline on Ru(0001) in vertical and two horizontal orientations, with both top-view and side-view visualizations. Adsorption energies show strongest binding for horizontal configurations (up to −3.59 eV), highlighting the strong interaction between aniline and the Ru surface.
summarization.b: illustrates aniline adsorption on Co(0001) for similar vertical and horizontal orientations. Although the geometries resemble those on Ru, the adsorption energies (−0.90 to −2.17 eV) reveal consistently weaker interaction than on Ru, indicating Co is less reactive toward aniline adsorption.
summarization.c: presents two weakly bound physisorption states of aniline on SiO₂(0001). With small adsorption energies (−0.39 and −0.57 eV), the molecule remains mostly undistorted and only loosely interacts with the oxide surface, consistent with SiO₂ being a growth-permissive rather than growth-blocking substrate.

d.question: Why is it important to compare aniline adsorption on Ru, Co, and SiO₂ when modeling selective-area ALD or ALE processes?
d.answer: Comparing adsorption on different surfaces reveals how strongly aniline can block growth on metals versus oxides. Strong adsorption on Ru and Co suggests effective surface inhibition, while weak adsorption on SiO₂ means the molecule desorbs easily, allowing selective growth on oxides. This contrast is essential for designing high-selectivity ALD/ALE processes where controlled deposition or removal depends on surface-dependent binding energies.

b.question: Which surface shows the strongest overall adsorption trend for aniline across all orientations?
b.answer: Ru(0001) shows the strongest adsorption, with ΔE_ads as low as −3.59 eV.

a.question: Does the adsorption orientation (vertical vs. horizontal) affect how ΔE_ads is computed in the DFT workflow?
a.answer: Yes each orientation requires a separate DFT geometry optimization before calculating ΔE_ads.

c.question: Which molecular or surface features most strongly influence adsorption strength (ΔE_ads)?
c.answer: π–d interaction between the benzene ring and the metal surface, Orientation of the aromatic ring relative to surface atoms, Metal surface reactivity (Ru > Co > SiO₂), N lone-pair interaction with surface metal atoms, Surface lattice coordination and available adsorption sites
